Section 17.7
REACTIONS OF ETHERS: A REVIEW AND A PREVIEW
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Reactivity of Ethers
Ethers are unreactive in general except in combustion reactions with O 2 . Ethers are highly flammable!
Ethers are oxidized slowly over time to explosive hydroperoxides
Hydrohalic acids can cleave ethers to alkyl halides (more in the next section)

Section 17.8
ACID-CATALYZED CLEAVAGE OF ETHERS

Acid-catalyzed Cleavage of Ethers
Hydrohalic acids protonate ethers, converting –OR to –(H) OR + (a good leaving group!)
Nucleophilic attack by halide ion X − produces an alkyl halide
Heat and excess of HX are typically used, causing a second nucleophilic substitution to occur

Mechanism
Step 1: protonation generates a good leaving group within the ether
Both carbons attached to O become electrophilic
The intermediate is “primed” for nucleophilic substitution

Mechanism-1
Step 2: halide ion attacks one of the electrophilic carbons; alcohol is formed as a leaving group
A similar mechanism leads to displacement of H 2 O from the alcohol by X −

Section 17.9
PREPARATION OF EPOXIDES

Two Methods for Epoxide Synthesis
There are two general methods used for synthesis of epoxides
Method 1: install an alcohol oxygen two atoms from an electrophilic carbon; perform intramolecular S N 2
Method 2: use an oxidant to establish both "C–O" bonds at once across a "C=C" π bond
Both start ultimately from an alkene "C=C"
Method 1 requires two steps but Method 2 only requires one
For both methods, the π bond must be a good nucleophile
We focus on Method 2 here and Method 1 in the next section

Epoxidation with Peracids
A peracid or peroxyacid can be used to prepare epoxides from alkenes with syn stereospecificity

Sharpless Epoxidation
The Sharpless epoxidation works on similar principles, but involves a hydroperoxide instead of a peracid and a transition-metal catalyst
This reaction is highly enantioselective and occurs stereospecifically with syn addition

Section 17.10
CONVERSION OF VICINAL HALOHYDRINS TO EPOXIDES

Epoxidation via Vicinal Halohydrin
Oxygen can act as a good nucleophile and alkyl halide carbon as an electrophile in S N 2
When the nucleophilic O and electrophilic C are connected via one C, an epoxide is the product!
Step 1: synthesis of vicinal halohydrin via addition of X and OH to alkene ( cohalogenation )

Epoxidation via Vicinal Halohydrin-1
Oxygen can act as a good nucleophile and alkyl halide carbon as an electrophile in S N 2
When the nucleophilic O and electrophilic C are connected via one C, an epoxide is the product!
Step 2: treatment with base to generate the alkoxide; spontaneous intramolecular S N 2
Stereochemistry of Epoxidation

Section 17.11
REACTIONS OF EPOXIDES WITH ANIONIC NUCLEOPHILES

Nucleophilic Substitution of Epoxides
Epoxides are unique among ethers: their carbons are highly electrophilic
Ring-opening provides a big incentive to substitution of C–O
A variety of anionic nucleophiles may be used, including RLi, RMgX, alkoxides, etc .

Regioselectivity and Stereoselectivity
The mechanisms of these reactions are universally S N 2
Substitution occurs with inversion of configuration at the electrophilic carbon
Substitution occurs preferentially at the less substituted electrophilic carbon

Section 17.12
ACID-CATALYZED RING OPENING OF EPOXIDES

Acid-catalyzed Nucleophilic Substitution
Acids can catalyze nucleophilic substitution of epoxides
These reactions do not involve a discrete carbocation intermediate—still S N 2 with inversion of configuration
Reaction occurs selectively at the more substituted position

Acid-catalyzed Nucleophilic Substitution-1
Acids can catalyze nucleophilic substitution of epoxides
These reactions do not involve a discrete carbocation intermediate—still S N 2 with inversion of configuration
Reaction occurs selectively at the more substituted position
Reaction occurs selectively at the carbon better able to support positive charge .

Application: anti Dihydroxylation
Acid-catalyzed epoxide opening with water ( hydrolysis ) leads to net anti dihydroxylation of alkenes
